title: Upon the Mountain
subtitle: Christ ascends the mountain and opens His mouth to teach the eight Beatitudes

stillness: When He teaches the religious and contemplative,
inviting to rest and to contemplation.
Let the spirit grow quiet before the Master upon the mountain.
gospel: After this the Lord Jesus made a sermon most beautiful and abundant.
He went up into a mountain with His disciples,
and sitting down, as a teacher,
He opened His mouth and taught them, saying:
Blessed are the poor in spirit;
for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
for they shall possess the earth.
behold: Behold the Lord ascending the mountain, that He might teach you the higher summits of the virtues.
Behold Him seated, as a teacher among His own,
and attend to each thing as if you were present.
Behold Him opening His mouth — the mouth that is the Word — and teaching.
He sets before you eight blessednesses,
and to each He fastens its own reward.
Hear Him name the blessed: the poor, the meek.
And know: no one can be found who does not wish to be blessed.
consider: Blessed are the poor in spirit — that is, by will and by choice, not by necessity nor by pretense.
Poverty of spirit is the full renouncing of all delightful things,
despising every delight that lies in riches, in pleasures, and in honors.
It is also the contempt of self, that even the good man count himself useless and beneath the rest.
Contempt of riches is born from contempt of self:
he who truly and humbly despises himself for God's sake easily despises also the temporal things that exist for self's sake.
He who does not care for himself within sets no great price on things without.
Poverty is the first perfection of those who would follow Christ, and the foundation of the whole spiritual building.
He cannot follow Christ unburdened who is loaded down with temporal things.
Nor is he free, but a slave, who bends the affection of his soul to these passing things;
for of the thing I love with attachment, I willingly make myself the slave.
Therefore nothing is to be loved but God Himself, or another thing for God's sake.
Let the greedy and the proud crave and love the kingdoms of the earth;
but blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven — now in hope, hereafter in reality.
After poverty follows meekness: for he who is poor is provoked by many injuries, and so it is needful that he be meek.
The meek man is gentle in heart, troubled by no kindling of the soul, but persevering always in goodness;
and patient in deed, bearing those who offend him, not rendering evil for evil.
Blessed are the meek, for they shall possess a twofold earth:
the earth of their own body, which they carry, having dominion over themselves;
and the earth of paradise, which they seek.
Then truly shall you possess the earth, when you cleave to Him who made heaven and earth.
For this is to be meek: not to resist your God.
prayer: who, that You might teach the higher summits of the virtues,
ascended the mountain with Your disciples,
and there taught the blessednesses and the sublime virtues,
and promised to each its fitting reward:
grant to me, frail as I am,
I may strive through the exercise of the virtues to have merit,
so that, in Your mercy, I may obtain also the reward.
Make me, who consider the reward, not refuse the work of the reward,
but let the hope of eternal salvation soften in me the pain of the present remedy,
and kindle my soul to the brightness of the work.
blessed with the blessedness of the way through grace,
and at the last blessed with the blessedness of the homeland through glory.

latinJewel.latin: Tunc vere possidebis terram, quando inhæseris ei, qui fecit cœlum et terram.
latinJewel.english: Then truly shall you possess the earth, when you cleave to Him who made heaven and earth.
